citation.title: Recent progress in thin film composite membranes based on the polymer of intrinsic microporosity PIM-1: Preparation, properties and performance

content: A B S T R A C T The glassy polymer of intrinsic microporosity PIM-1 exhibits characteristics such as high free volume, reasonablegas pair selectivities, organophilic properties, stability in many organic solvents and good solution processability.It is thus an excellent membrane material that is widely studied for both gas and liquid separation. Whereasmuch research has focused on relatively thick (>20 μm) self-standing membranes at laboratory scale, thin filmcomposite (TFC) membranes provide much less transmembrane mass transfer resistance due to their thin selective layer (<2 μm), making them attractive for industrial applications at large scale. However, the propertiesof thin films usually deviate from those of thick films. In particular, the polymer solution can penetrate into theunderlying layer during the coating process, resulting in the formation of an interpenetration layer, whichsignificantly affects the performance of the TFC membranes. In this review, research on PIM-1 based TFCmembranes is critically and comprehensively examined. Key factors influencing TFC separation performance,including membrane preparation methods, support properties, polymer characteristics and interfacial properties,are thoroughly discussed, in order to provide valuable insights and guidelines for fabricating stable and optimalperforming PIM-1 based TFC membranes.

content: 1. Introduction Relatively thick freestanding membranes are widely applied at thelaboratory scale for the characterization of membrane materials. However, the high mass transfer resistance makes them unsuitable for industrial applications. The concept of an integrally skinned asymmetricmembrane was first proposed by Loeb and Sourirajan in the 1960s [1].This type of membrane consists of a thin skin layer and a porous supportlayer formed of the same material, which is usually prepared by eithernonsolvent-induced phase separation [2] or thermally induced phaseseparation techniques [3]. An asymmetric structure provides a fast flowrate without sacrificing separation performance (e.g., selectivity, molecular weight cut-off and rejection), which is suitable for commercialuse. However, the application of integrally skinned asymmetric membranes is limited by the small number of polymer materials that can usethis fabrication method, and by the potential high cost of full membranefabrication with expensive polymer materials. The first generation of thin film composite (TFC) polyamide membranes was invented byCadotte in the late 1970s using an interfacial polymerization method,and TFC membranes now dominate the global desalination market [4].TFC membranes are typically composed of three to four layers, as shownin Fig.

content: method,and TFC membranes now dominate the global desalination market [4].TFC membranes are typically composed of three to four layers, as shownin Fig. 1: (i) A support layer, usually comprised of a non-woven fabriclayer with micron scale pores and an intermediate polymeric membranelayer of nanometer size pores, which provides mechanical strength withminimum transmembrane resistance; (ii) a gutter layer, which is anoptional layer that enhances the flux through the membrane, improvesthe compatibility between the support and selective layer, and/or prevents the casting solution from penetrating into the support layer; (iii)an interpenetration layer, which may form during TFC membranepreparation when the coating solution penetrates into either the gutterlayer or support layer [5–7]; this may range from a few nanometers tohundreds of nanometers depending on the support pore size and viscosity of the coating solution; (iv) the active layer, the key layer in TFCmembranes, usually with thickness less than 2 μm, which provides the

content: Journal of Membrane Science 722 (2025) 123844 actual separation performance. Sometimes a protective layer may becoated on top of the active layer to seal defects [8] or suppress plasticization [9]. Each layer (except the interpenetration layer) can be madefrom different materials, which allow the materials to be optimizedindependently. These multilayered structures may be formed as eitherflat sheets or hollow fibers. Hollow fiber membranes (HFMs) are widelyapplied in industry due to their high surface area-to-volume ratio andexcellent scalable production [10]. However, in research laboratoriesthe focus is often on flat sheet membranes, which are convenient tofabricate and easy to characterize.Polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) are a class of highly glassypolymers, which were first reported by Budd and McKeown in 2004[11]. The archetypal PIM-1 contains fully fused rings interconnected byspiro centers in the polymer backbone, which restricts the free rotationof polymer chains to form a highly rigid and contorted polymericstructure with intrinsic micropores (dimensions less than 2 nm). Thesecharacteristics provide PIM-1 with high free volume and high gaspermeability. The excellent solubility in normal organic solvents such astetrahydrofuran (THF) and chloroform (CHCl3) endows PIM-1 with goodprocessibility as a membrane material [12].

citation.title: Recent progress in thin film composite membranes based on the polymer of intrinsic microporosity PIM-1: Preparation, properties and performance

content: ility in normal organic solvents such astetrahydrofuran (THF) and chloroform (CHCl3) endows PIM-1 with goodprocessibility as a membrane material [12]. PIM-1 is mainly studied forapplications in gas separation, including post-combustion carbon capture, natural gas sweetening and air separation, due to ultrahigh gaspermeabilities and reasonable gas selectivities for gas pairs such asCO2/N2, CO2/CH4, and O2/N2 [13–15]. Furthermore, chemical modifications of PIM-1, including amidation [16], carboxylation [7,17], amidoximation [18], amination [19,20], sulfonation [21] and crosslinking[22,23], can improve gas separation performance. However, not allmodifications are useful for the formation of TFC membranes. Sometypes of pre-modified PIM-1 lose solution processibility, and the harshconditions required for post-modification of PIM-1 based TFC membranes may cause film delamination and failure. Most literature onPIM-based TFC membranes involves PIM-1 or its carboxylated modification, cPIM-1.In the liquid separation area, PIM-1 membranes were first reported in2004 to remove phenol from aqueous solution by pervaporation [12].Their organophilic nature and stability to many organic solventsencouraged investigation of the separation of alcohol-water systems andorganic-organic mixtures using pervaporation [15,24].

content: y to many organic solventsencouraged investigation of the separation of alcohol-water systems andorganic-organic mixtures using pervaporation [15,24]. In organic solvent nanofiltration, PIM-1 membranes with intrinsic micropores caneffectively retain small molecules but allow high solvent permeability.However, the high affinity of PIM-1 to organic solvents causes significant polymer swelling, which enlarges inter-chain distances, increasesthe membrane flux, but decreases the rejection of target molecules [25].To inhibit membrane swelling and maintain the separation performance, modification of PIM-1 is required to introduce interactions between chains. Further crosslinking can help to build more stablestructures [26].This review specifically focuses on recent advances in research onPIM-1 based TFC membranes. Thin film properties such as physicalaging, plasticization, film swelling, and thermal properties are discussedin detail. Furthermore, this review discusses the main factors that affectthe separation performance of PIM-1 based TFC membranes (e.g.,membrane preparation method, support properties, interfacial properties and polymer properties). Guidelines are provided for fabricatingstable and well-performing PIM-1 based TFC membranes.

content: 2. Thin film composite membrane preparation techniques 2.1. Coating strategy 2.1.1. Dip-coating and kiss-coatingDip-coating is one of the most commonly used coating methods forTFC membrane preparation at laboratory scale (Fig. 2a), usually as abatch coating process. The procedure can be summarized as: i) Affixsupport onto a firm substrate (e.g., glass plate, stainless steel plate), withthe edges sealed to prevent solution from soaking to the back of thesupport; ii) immerse support into polymer solution for time intervalsranging from a few seconds to several minutes, and then withdraw;immersion and withdrawal steps may need to go through several cycles;iii) drain extra solution gravitationally; iv) evaporate solvent. Dipcoating is applicable for both flat sheet membranes and hollow fibermembranes [27].Industrial scale dip-coating (Fig. 2b) can be modified into a continuous process [28,29]. The support passes through a roller conveyancedevice, encountering a polymer solution with a certain residence time.However, a relatively long residence time in the dip-coating method ismore likely to lead to solution penetration. The withdrawing speed alsoaffects the performance of fabricated TFC membranes [28].

Furthermore, for continuous dip-coating it is hard to protect the back of a flatsheet support from solution soaking.As a variation of the continuous dip-coating technique, the kisscoating method is realized through controlling the contact betweenthe coating solution and the support by the surface tension. The kisscoating method (Fig. 2c) was first reported by Dibrov et al. [30] in2014. Subsequently, Alberto et al. [31] extended its application to PIM-1in 2018 for pervaporation, although this technique was referred to as’dip-coating’ in the literature. Borisov et al. [6] then applied this technique and used the term “kiss-coating” in the preparation of PIM-1 TFCmembranes for gas separation. The motion of a stainless-steel roller isdriven by a motor connected to a power supply. The support is placedonto the roller with sealed taped edges, and the interaction between thesupport and the coating solution is controlled via surface tension, whichhelps to minimize the active layer thickness. The selection of the coatingspeed (a combination of the coater size and power supply voltage) alsoaffectsthefilmperformance.Thekiss-coatingmethodensuressingle-sided coating and significantly reduces residence time (less than1 s), resulting in TFC membranes that are more uniform andreproducible. 2.1.2. Spin-coatingSpin-coating (Fig. 2d) stands out as a straightforward and convenienttechnique for producing active layers down to 200 nm on a laboratoryscale, which is challenging for other coating methods [32,33]. Thesupport membrane, adhered to a substrate, is vacuum attached to aplatform. A specific amount of coating solution is poured onto thesupport membrane, then rotation is initiated immediately. The typicalrotational speed is usually in the range of 500–2000 rpm. Excess solutionis ejected under the influence of centrifugal forces, while the remainingsolution, after solvent evaporation, forms the active layer. The use ofcentrifugal forces typically facilitates the generation of thinner and moreuniform active layers compared to other coating methods. The activelayer thickness can be regulated by varying the concentration of coatingsolution and spin speed. However, the inability to scale up for industrial

content: Journal of Membrane Science 722 (2025) 123844 production and the wastage of coating solutions are crucial limitations. 2.1.3. Electrospray-coatingThe electrospray-coating (Fig. 2e) process involves dispersing acoating solution into micron sized droplets under a strong electric fieldand depositing them onto a support. Subsequently, a thin film forms asthe solvent evaporates. Electrospray-coating offers the benefits of precise control of film thickness and industrial scalability. The McCutcheongroup applied an electrospray-coating method to fabricate polyamidedesalination TFC membranes in 2018 [34] and later achieved successfulfabrication of TFC membranes with PIM-1 [35]. The support membranewas affixed to a rotating drum wrapped with aluminum foil. A certaindistance was maintained between the syringe needle and the drum, anda high electric voltage (approximately 6.8 kV) was applied. A syringepump was utilized to control the flow rate of the coating solution. Underthe influence of the high electric voltage and relatively low concentration, the solution was dispersed into small droplets, depositing onto thesurface of the support membrane to form a TFC membrane. Severalcoating cycles were required to minimize defects. However, this technique still requires further optimization as the TFC membranes preparedthrough this method exhibit marginally lower gas permeance and selectivities, compared to those prepared using other coating methods. 2.1.4.

Doctor blade or bar-coatingDoctor blade or bar-coating (Fig. 2f) is a simple coating method andwidely used in thin film preparation, but its application for PIM-1 TFCmembranes is seldom reported [36]. Firstly, excess coating solution ispoured onto the substrate, then a film applicator bar or blade is used tospread the solution evenly across the surface to form a liquid film. Timeis allowed for solvent evaporation to form the final TFC membranes.Though this method is fast and scalable, and film thickness is controlledaccurately through the distance between the applicator and the substrate, it is likely constrained by the typically low concentration andviscosity of the PIM-1 solution and the requirement for a high boilingpoint solvent [37].

content: 2.2. Spinning method Hollow fiber membranes (HFMs) are widely used in industrial applications due to several advantages over flat sheet membranes, such asa high surface area-to-volume ratio, self-supporting capability, scalability, and flexibility [38]. HFMs can be prepared similarly to flat sheetmembranes by dipping porous hollow fiber supports into coating solutions [27,39], a technique often employed at the laboratory scale.HFMs can also be fabricated through a spinning process that forms acontinuous length of fiber through extrusion of a polymer dope througha spinneret. For PIM-1 based HFMs, a triple orifice spinneret is typicallyrequired [40,41], where the bore fluid, inner dope, and outer dope areco-extruded into a coagulation bath through an air gap, driven byhigh-pressure syringe pumps, and then collected by a roller collector, asshown in Fig. 2g. The bore fluid (solvent) helps maintain the hollowfiber structure, preventing collapse, while the inner dope (polymer solution) forms the membrane, and the outer dope (non-solvent) regulatesmembrane morphology. Several parameters, such as the compositionand flow rates of the dopes, the air gap distance, and the collectionspeed, need to be optimized during the spinning process to achievedesired membrane characteristics.

content: 2.3. Interfacial polymerization In addition to coating and spinning techniques, interfacial polymerization presents itself as an appealing method for the fabrication ofTFC membranes, as the in situ crosslinking can lead to the formation ofextremely thin layers (<150 nm) [42]. However, direct interfacialpolymerization of conventional PIMs is challenging. Therefore, currentresearch primarily emphasizes integrating the intrinsic microporosityproperties into a polyarylate polymer. This is often achieved through theincorporation of spiro-centered monomers, such as 5,5′,6,6′ tetrahydroxy-3,3,3′,3′-tetramethylspirobisindane (TTSBI), 9,9-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)fluorene [43–45] or PIM oligomers [46].Similar to the conventional interfacial polymerization method usedfor polyamide TFC membranes, the methodology consists of: i) Firstly,impregnate the support membrane with an organic solvent containingtrimesoyl chloride (TMC) and drain excess solution; ii) pour an aqueoussolution containing a phenoxide version of an appropriate spirocentered monomer onto the substrate surface; iii) after time for reaction, pour off the excess aqueous solution; iv) condition the crosslinkedmembrane at a specific temperature and humidity.

These advantages make it an attractive option forlarge-scale manufacturing, ensuring efficiency and cost-effectivenesswhile maintaining membrane performance. 3. Thin film properties 3.1. Physical aging Like all other glassy polymers, PIMs suffer from significant physicalaging, or structure relaxation [47], which is one of the main reasons thatimpede their application in industry. The polymer tends to densify towards a theoretical equilibrium state over time (Fig. 3a) [48], leading todisappearance of micropores (>6.5 Å) [49], reduction in free volumeand a decline in gas permeability [50], but usually accompanied byincreased gas selectivity in line with the Robeson upper bound (trade-offbetween permeability and selectivity) [51]. The structural changesduring physical aging are reflected in the scattering patterns characterized by X-ray diffraction analysis (Fig. 3b) [52]. Physical aging tendsto be very fast in the early stages but slows down afterwards and isthickness dependent [53–55]. The permeability reduction that typicallytakes a few months in a relatively thick membrane (around 50 μm) mayonly require a few days in a thin film state (<2 μm), as shown in Fig. 3c[7].

This may be linked to higher segmental mobility near free surfaces[56], leading to faster structural rearrangement and densification.Surface constraints can mitigate this effect. Murphy et al. [57]employed a co-extrusion technique to fabricate multilayered polysulfone (PSF) thin films with rubbery polymers serving as spacing materials. Surprisingly, the multilayered PSF thin films, comprisinghundreds of thin layers ranging in thickness from 180 to 400 nm, butfeaturing only two free surfaces, exhibited a similar aging rate to bulkfilms with a thickness of 60–80 μm. This suggests that the presence offree surfaces plays a crucial role in the aging of thin films, not merelythickness [58].Liquid methanol treatment is generally considered as an effectivemethod to rejuvenate aged PIM-1 membranes [59]. The swellingresulting from methanol treatment can enhance the mobility of polymerchains, restoring lost free volume due to physical aging [60]. However,the additional free volume obtained from methanol treatment results ina stronger driving force towards the equilibrium state [61–63], therebyaccelerating the aging process in the early stage. While liquid methanol treatment can rejuvenate thickmembranes that have aged, its applicability to thin films is limited.Cracks are prone to develop shortly after removing thin films from themethanol bath, and the thin film durability is also affected [55].Furthermore, this method is not applicable in the gas separation industry because typical membrane gas separation modules are notdesigned to deal with liquids [64]. It is suggested that some branchedstructures [68], including small loops [67] may help to “hold” thepolymericstructureanddecreaseagingrate.Comparedwithself-standing thin films, the physical aging behavior of a TFC membraneis complex due to the nature and evolution of the interpenetration between the active layer and support layer upon aging, which will bediscussed below. A very thick interpenetration layer created as aconsequence of excessive solution penetration often leads to faster agingperformance and less selective membranes [65].Enhanced polymer rigidity has been explored as an approach toovercome the aging effect [69]. However, the anti-aging efficacy mayface challenges upon integration into TFC membranes [70], due to theincreased brittleness of the membrane, which makes it more prone todeveloping defects during the aging process. An alternative is to introduce fillers to form mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) to impede polymer densification [71], thereby enhancing the aging resistance of TFCmembranes [72]. Improving interfacial compatibility, however, mightbe crucial for maintaining a more stable performance over time [73]. 3.2.

Plasticization and swelling Plasticization is another concern in membrane-based gas separation.The swelling of the membrane, induced by the high solubility of penetrating components such as CO2, hydrocarbons, and aromatics, enhanceschain mobility and increases the fractional free volume of the membrane, leading to an increase in membrane permeability but drop inselectivity [74]. Plasticization typically manifests at a specific pressurein relatively thick PIM membranes. However, in thin films, the pressureat which plasticization occurs is reduced [75], and an immediate permeance increase might be observed as pressure increases [55], as shownin Fig. 4a.

content: Ellipsometry is a powerful tool to monitor the changes in opticalproperties of thin films swelling under pressure [76,77]. Ogieglo et al.[78] studied the CO2 sorption behavior in very thin PIM-1 films withthicknesses range from 7 nm to 128 nm. The swelling behavior of thethin film, with a swelling degree of 2–2.5 % under pressures of 3–9 bar,was primarily attributed to micropore filling, followed by a dissolutionprocess at higher pressures (Fig. 4b). Thinner films tend to swell moreeasily to relax stress induced by CO2 sorption, corresponding to adecrease in the nominal glass transition temperature (Tg). Ogieglo et al.[79] further studied the dilation and sorption behavior of CO2 and CH4in PIM-1 thin films (~500 nm) and ultrathin films (~50 nm). Comparedwith CO2, the solubility of a larger and less condensable gas molecule,CH4, was more strongly correlated with film thickness. Thinner filmstended to have lower concentrations of dissolved gas due to the reduction of fractional free volume. Yu et al. [80] have shown that PIM-1 andcPIM-1 TFC membranes start to plasticize at a CO₂ pressure of approximately 0.8–1 bar.

content: table support materials constrainthebroaderapplicationofthesetechniquesinTFCmembranes. 4. Gas separation performance of PIM-1 based TFC membranes 4.1. Factors affecting TFC membrane gas separation performance The gas separation performance of PIM-1 based TFC membranesdepends upon many factors such as the support characteristics, solvent,and the concentration of the coating solution (Fig. 6a) [65]. Polymericsupports, including polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) [31,93] and polyacrylonitrile (PAN) [25,94], and inorganic supports such as porousalumina [32,33] can be used. Due to its excellent compatibility withPIMs and stability with commonly used casting solvents (THF andCHCl3) [7], ultrafiltration grade PAN has become the most widely utilized support material.The pore size, porosity and surface roughness are the three maincharacteristics of the available substrates. High porosity usually meanslow transmembrane resistance. However, the resistance of the support isalways far less than the selective layer, so this is not a critical factor. Poresize holds greater significance, as a larger pore size (>50 nm) [95]typically leads to substantial solution penetration and insufficientretention of casting solution on the surface for the formation of adefect-free thin layer, leading to a non-selective TFC membrane [28]. Asmaller pore size helps in retaining the solution on the surface.

Generally, support pore size ranges from 20 nm to 30 nm would be consideredto be ideal for TFC membrane preparation, based on literature reports[25,28,32,94,96]. Otherwise, a gutter layer may be incorporated [72,97–99] and/or the support may be pre-wetted with water [100] tomitigate solution penetration. However, incorporation of a gutter layerin TFC membrane fabrication entails an additional processing step, andthere are concerns about the introduction of water because of the potential challenges in removing it from both the support layer [101] andPIM-1 layer [13].

content: The polymer coating solution can have a similar impact to differingsupport pore sizes. Both solution concentration and polymer characteristics (such as polymer molar mass, network content and topology)influence solution viscosity. In general, a low-viscosity solution tends topenetrate more, whereas a high-viscosity solution is better retained onthe surface, but often leads to the formation of a thicker active layer [93,100]. A support with a smaller average pore size can withstand a lowerconcentration, leading to a thinner active layer with less penetration,and potential savings in polymer usage. An interpenetration layer withinthe support contributes to additional gas permeation resistance, resulting in a film with a lower apparent active layer thickness, but exhibitinga lower gas permeability than expected [65,102]. However, solutionpenetration can also have positive effects, helping to anchor the activelayer to the support and enhancing TFC membrane stability. The gasselectivity of TFC membranes may be enhanced, compared to theirthicker parent self-standing films, due to a better size sieving effect fromthe interpenetration layer, as the transport of non-condensable gases issignificantly impeded by the decreased chain mobility of polymerswithin the pores, whereas condensable gases remain less affected [5].Direct contact between the coating solution and the PAN support canlead to a thick (>500 nm) interpenetration layer (Fig.

Borisov et al. [6] coated PIM-1 TFC membranes on substratesconsisting of a cross-linked poly[1-trimethylsilyl-1-propyne] (PTMSP)gutter layer and a highly porous microfiltration support layer. Theyachieved unusually high CO2/N2 selectivity (approximately 35–55) inthin films, compared to the selectivity in PIM-1 thick films (around12–16), which they attributed to the presence of a 5 nm interpenetrationlayer at the interface between the selective and gutter layers. It iscommon for commercially available ultrafiltration supports to havedefects or pinholes. Although these imperfections do not significantlyimpact filtration performance, they can deteriorate the coating process,resulting in active layer defects forming around those areas. Qiu et al.[99] deposited graphene oxide using vacuum filtration on a PAN support.

In this case, the gas wasintroduced initially into the support layer before passing through thepolyurea (PU)/PIM-1 layer. Such TFC membranes achieved bothelevated gas permeance and selectivity.The topology of PIM-1 significantly influences the long-term performance of PIM-1 based TFC membranes. Foster et al. [66] reportedtopologically different PIM-1 polymers by regulating the polymerizationtemperature profiles and N2 flow with a tetrachloro monomer (TCTPN,tetrachloroterephthalonitrile), in contrast to the traditionally used tetrafluoro monomer (TFTPN, tetrafluoroterephthalonitrile). Originatingfrom mono-substitution in the early stage of step growth polymerizations, branched PIM-1 offers diversity in polymer microstructures,including branched, tadpole and cyclic structures, as shown in Fig. 8a.However, branching can further develop into insoluble networks ifinsufficient N2 purging is applied. PIM-1 containing large amounts ofsuch colloidal network is not ideal for preparing TFC membranes andusually suffers from an inability to form a defect-free active layer orlong-term stability issues [67].

Branching can be identified using protonnuclear magnetic resonance, appearing as smaller shoulder peaksadjacent to the main aromatic proton peaks. It should be noted thatPIM-1 prepared from TFTPN can also contains branched structures,depending on the temperature profile [7], but the molar mass is usuallyhigher (>100 kg/mol) than samples prepared from TCTPN.The creation of branching units in the early stages of step growthpolymerization with TCTPN promotes the formation of small loops(Fig. 8b), which limits the overall molar mass of the polymer formed.Branched polymer chains can counteract polymer densification to someextent. The densification of PIM-1 residues within small, closed-loopstructures is expected to be significantly more restricted compared tothat of a solely di-substituted polymeric structure during the agingprocess of a TFC membrane. The existence of small loops in a PIM-1polymer with relatively low molar mass has proved to slow down theaging rate compared to a predominantly di-substituted PIM-1 in TFCmembrane applications [67]. For example, PIM-1 TFC membranesdeveloped by Foster et al. [67] and Almansour et al. [109] exhibited areduction in CO2 permeance of 85 % in four months and of 73 % in ayear, respectively.

PIM-1 samples that contain, or are mixed with,branched colloidal networks can show limited aging in the mediumterm, but followed with a rapid drop in CO2 permeance. Furthermore, asignificant drop in selectivity may be observed, probably due to mesopores formed at interfaces between the continuous phase and thenetwork phase, due to different aging rates [67,110].The aging of PIM-1 TFC membranes does not always follow the expected trade-off between permeance and selectivity. In many cases,substantial decreases in permeance are not accompanied by corresponding improvements in selectivity. Tension originating from polymerdensification tends to contract a thick PIM-1 film in all dimensions.However, interaction between PIM-1 thin films with the underlying PANsupport, or perhaps with an interpenetration layer, may lead to theformation of nonselective mesopores or defects, playing a role indecreased selectivity [111]. Such phenomena were not observed in theaging of very thin but self-supported PIM-1 films [55]. It is also possiblethat an open branched structure offers more flexibility to overcome theinteraction with the support compared to a rigid linear structure [110].Considerable research has focused on TFC membranes prepared from

content: 4.2. Archetypal PIM-1 TFC membranes Self-standing thick PIM-1 membranes have been extensively investigated since the first gas permeation studies in 2005 [112]. A considerable scatter of gas permeabilities and selectivities have been reportedfrom different studies, due to differences in polymer topology, andcasting and drying conditions. When PIM-1 samples are processed intoTFC membranes, these inherent differences are significantly magnified.Additionally, the post-treatment, storage conditions and film thicknesscan also significantly affect thin film properties [113]. Consequently, thegas separation performances of PIM-1 TFC membranes reported in theliterature show a wide disparity, as CO2 permeance can range from a fewhundred GPU to nearly 20,000 GPU and CO2/N2 selectivities may varyfrom 10 to 55. Gas separation performances of all fresh and aged PIM-1related TFC membranes are summarized in Fig. 9, with Table 1 presenting information about thin film preparation, properties and freshseparation performance. In general, when processed into TFC membranes, PIM-1 often struggles to meet the requirements for industrialpost-combustion carbon capture (shaded area Fig. 9a). Furthermore, theaging of these membranes results in both decreased permeance andselectivity (Fig. 9b). Only after some modifications are applied, such asthe integration of nanoparticles to form thin film nanocomposite (TFN)membranes, separation performance in the target area can be achieved(Fig.

Optimization to 2 wt% filler loading led to a 34 %increase in CO2 permeance and 17 % increase in CO2/N2 selectivity.Long term aging also benefited from disrupted polymer densification,exhibiting a decline of only 13 % in O2 permeance after 14 days.Graphene oxide (GO) and its derivatives, with their two-dimensional(2D) structure and high aspect ratio, also draw great interest. Aliyevet al. [94] investigated the effect of GO and functionalized GO on TFCmembrane performance. Compared to pristine GO, functionalized fillers(Fig. 11b) exhibited improved compatibilities with the PIM-1 matrix andboosted gas transport, resulting in higher gas permeances and selectivities. However, with a further increase in filler loading, permeance andselectivity typically decreased, with severe defects forming when loadings were over 50 wt%. Conversely, small amounts of GO filler couldremarkably improve the performance. Almansour et al. [109] modifiedGO to produce amine-functionalized reduced holey graphene oxide(rHGO) and explored filler concentrations within the range of 0.1–5 wt

citation.title: Recent progress in thin film composite membranes based on the polymer of intrinsic microporosity PIM-1: Preparation, properties and performance

content: %. The addition of just 0.1 wt% filler resulted in a remarkable increase ofover 100 % in CO2 permeance. Furthermore, incorporating 1 wt% of thefiller effectively slowed down film aging, with CO2 permeancedecreasing by only 19 % over the course of a year, contributing fromenhanced intermolecular forces of the MMM system between thebranched PIM-1 and functionalized fillers.Another type of 2D material, porous silica nanosheets (SN), has alsobeen reported for this application (Fig. 11c) [118]. Nevertheless, onlyexfoliated fillers with amination or sulfonation, applied at low concentrations, demonstrated improved gas separation performance, emphasizing the crucial role of interfacial compatibility. The most effectiveloading was determined to be 0.05 wt%, exhibiting enhanced CO2 permeance and CO2/CH4 selectivity. However, a decrease in selectivity wasobserved when films aged from day 7 to day 28, as the base PIM-1polymer was possibly not branched enough to maximize the interactions with fillers.The organic-inorganic hybrid nanomaterial, polyhedral oligomericsilsesquioxane (POSS), with a cage-like structure and tunable functionality, was also studied as a filler for PIM-1 TFN membranes [32]. Aminefunctionalized POSS showed better dispersion.

content: ike structure and tunable functionality, was also studied as a filler for PIM-1 TFN membranes [32]. Aminefunctionalized POSS showed better dispersion. The addition of this filleronly helped to increase CO2/N2 selectivity by 24 %, accompanied by aCO2 permeance drop of 11 % at 5 wt% loading, but noticeable agingmitigation effects observed in thick films were not reflected in these TFCmembranes.MOFs are also a popular category of fillers due to their tunability,large surface area and porous nature [126,127]. In membrane separation, MOFs offer additional gas transport pathways and molecularsieving capabilities, simultaneously enhancing gas permeance andselectivity. Liu et al. [72] synthesized two types of MOF, MOF-74-Ni andNH2-UiO-66. PIM-1 TFN membranes prepared with 10 wt% MOF-74-Niloading improved CO2 permeance by 16 % and CO2/N2 selectivity by 63%, respectively, and maintained a better aging performance than theoriginal PIM-1 TFC membranes (Fig. 12). These results could be attributed to the increased free volume and formation of highly CO2 selectiveinterfaces due to the strong interaction between uncoordinated metal(Ni2+) sites and –OH groups with PIM-1. On the other hand,NH2-UiO-66, despite showing a higher improvement in CO2 permeance(73 %), experienced poorer aging performance compared to MOF-74-Ni,possibly due to weaker interactions with PIM-1. Fluorine modification ofUiO-66 yielded a more hydrophobic UiO-66-(CF3)2 [120].

Other modifications of PIM-1 based TFC membranes Although various chemical modifications have been carried out onPIM-1, such as amine modification [128], amidoxime modification[129], amide modification [130], sulfonation [21], carboxylation [17]and so on, only a few modified PIM-1s show sufficient solution processibility for the fabrication of TFC membranes. As one example, TFCmembranes of branched, acid-hydrolyzed PIM-1 (B-cPIM-1), with carboxylic acid functionality, exhibited a nearly 300 % increase in idealCO2/N2 selectivity while maintaining a similar CO2 permeance level asthe parent branched PIM-1 (B-PIM-1), at around 3000 GPU [7].Benefiting from the branched structure, the CO2 permeance level wasalso quite stable over 60 days. However, the mixed gas performanceproved to be poor, due to a strong plasticization effect, which indicatedthe importance of studying TFC membranes under mixed gas conditions.Alshurafa et al. [95] prepared TFN membranes of cPIM-1 crosslinkedwith Niand Co-MOF-74. Nanosized MOFs prepared this way showedbetter compatibility and dispersion with cPIM-1 than that achieved withunmodified PIM-1 by direct blending and grafting methods, manifestingas better gas separation performance.

content: Liang et al. [27] synthesized a PIM-1 copolymer, PIM-CD, incorporatingthe bulky beta-cyclodextrin group into PIM-1. Optimal PIM-CD TFCmembranes showed 20 % more CO2 permeance than PIM-1 and a 6 %increase in CO2/N2 selectivity. However, when compared to the 67 %increase in CO2 permeability observed in the self-standing membranestate, there is still room for further boosting the performance. Similarly,Liu et al. [70] prepared PIM-C1 with higher free volume though a postspirobisindane locking modification. TFC membranes spin coated fromPIM-C1 showed 47 % higher CO2 permeance than PIM-1 TFC membranes with similar active layer thickness, but there was still a significant gap compared to the approximately 120 % CO2 permeabilityimprovement in the thick film state. Furthermore, the stability ofPIM-C1 TFC membranes remained a problem, showing low CO2 permeance (220 GPU) and CO2/N2 selectivity (~5.5) compared to theoriginal 6400 and 29, after only two weeks of aging. It is possible thatthe more highly rigid structure of PIM-C1 is more prone to developnon-selective mesopores during aging, as mentioned earlier. Both worksemphasized the complexity and difficulty of the TFC system, which requires more effort for understanding and optimization.PIM-1 is a good carrier for ionic liquids and can be used in thefabrication of ionic liquid supported membranes (ILSMs). Han et al.[102] successfully made Bmim2Co(NCS)@PIM-1 TFC membranes for airseparation.

Due to the partial miscibility of PIM-1 with these polymers,solutions containing only 5–15 wt% of PIM-1 were successfully preparedand processed. Though thin activelayers (thickness below 100 nm) were realized, the hollow fiber membranes presented mainly the bulk polymer properties, with enhancement by the addition of PIM-1 component, but generally much lower gaspermeance than pure PIM-1 based TFC membranes.

Additionally,improving the performance of the parent polymer is crucial to provide ahigh starting point. content: oration PIM-1 based TFC membranes have been studied for the separation ofliquid mixtures by pervaporation, in which the permeate is removed as avapor. Membrane performance is often quoted in terms of flux andseparation factor (β), which is defined as the composition ratio at thepermeate side relative to that at the feed side. The pervaporation performance of PIM-1 related TFC membranes is collected in Table 2. Gaoet al. [93] evaluated the performance in organophilic pervaporation of abutanol/water binary mixture. PIM-1 TFC membranes were prepared bydip-coating on homemade PVDF supports with different surface properties, and the optimized support with highest surface porosity (69 %)resulted in the highest total flux of 9.08 kg m-2 h-1 and a reasonable β of

This was attributed to the influence of concentrationpolarization at higher fluxes and to thickness dependent film propertiessuch as free volume. It is also possible that an interpenetration layer wasformed as supports with quite a large average pore size were used in thiswork. Solution penetration typically results in a reduced nominal activelayer thickness but additional mass transfer resistance. Alberto et al.[31] subsequently aimed to further enhance the pervaporation performance by introducing functionalized reduced graphene oxide (rGO)fillers. However, due to the low surface porosity of the PVDF support(4.2–11 %) utilized in this study, the reference PIM-1 TFC membranesonly exhibited a total flux of 4.3 kg m-2 h-1 and β of 12.7. The additionof 0.05 wt% of nanosized octylamine functionalized rGO (rGO-OA) didlead to improvements, increasing the total flux by 35 % and β by 34 %,respectively. However, up to 8 h of probe sonication was required toensure a good dispersion. Benefiting from the alkyl chain of rGO-OAfillers, the swelling of the PIM-1 matrix was better controlled thanwith other fillers, helping to maintain a relatively high β value.Contreras et al. Consequently, the active layers become thicker, leading toperformances closer to that of self-standing membranes [93,100]. Organic solvent nanofiltration PIM-1 membranes are prone to swelling by organic solvents, leadingto an increase in solvent flux and molecular weight cut-off in organicsolvent nanofiltration (OSN), as indicated in Fig. 15a. It is important tocontrol membrane swelling and maintain a good compromise betweenflux and rejection. Fritsch et al. [25] reported the application of PIM-1based TFC membranes to recover heptane from a feed containing hexaphenylbenzene (HPB) by nanofiltration. However, the neat PIM-1 layersuffered from swelling by the heptane, resulting in fluxes range from 3 to7 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 with a HPB rejection range from 87 % to 92 %. Thebest performing TFC membrane was prepared by crosslinking PIM-1with polyethylenimine under 120 ◦C heat treatment for 16 h, with aPDMS gutter layer on a PAN support; the flux decreased to 2.4 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, but the rejection increased to 97 %.Researchers are often keen to produce thinner films in order toobtain higher flux but, interestingly, it has been shown that film permeance is not always inversely proportional to film thickness [83].When the film thickness is less than 140 nm it is difficult for the intrinsicmicroporosity of PIM-1 to be retained and the overall structure tends tocollapse, leading to a reduction in free volume and film permeability(Fig. 15b).

Intriguingly, annealing up to 150 ◦C does not affect thenanofiltration performance of PIM-1 thin films, despite acceleratingtheir aging, manifested in gas separation [90]. To further improve theflux of TFC membranes, the Zhu group [103,104] developed a SVAprocess (Fig. 6d). By storing PIM-1 TFC membranes in THF vapor for lessthan 5 min, immediately after dip-coating, the polymer tended topenetrate further into the support, leading to a thinner active layer but athicker interpenetration layer. Nevertheless, the incorporation of PIM-1into the support did not lead to an overall increase in mass transferresistance; instead, it further enhanced the ethanol flux. However, aprolonged treatment time resulted in defect formation and decreasedmolecular sieving ability. Furthermore, the addition of nano-TiO2 fillersuppressed PIM-1 aging by acting as a physical crosslinker, maintaininga more stable ethanol flux over time. Additionally, a liquid methanoltreatment was feasible to rejuvenate thin films from swelling caused bytoluene [28].Crosslinking is an effective method to increase the stability ofmembranes in organic solvents, and various crosslinking techniques forthin films have been developed. content: Thermal crosslinking of PIM-1 was considered an effective method totune the micropore structures and molecular sieving properties [23], butonly high temperature tolerant materials (e.g. ceramic α-Al2O3) could beused as supports [91]. As commonly occurs with most cross-linkingmethods, the solvent flux dropped, but the thermally crosslinkedPIM-1 showed significant stability in acetone and dimethyl formamide(DMF), whether in storage or continuous operation. However, a scalingup of the dip-coating method resulted in a slight decrease in rejection.The scale-up also increased the number of defective membranes andresulted in active layers that were twice the thickness.Amidoxime-functionalized PIM-1 (AOPIM-1), a modified version ofPIM-1, was employed in nanofiltration as TFC membranes [26]. Afterspin-coating, it was preferential to soak the membranes in ethanol toexchange the DMF solvent and remove oligomers, bringing about anincrease in free volume and nearly 100 % increase in ethanol permeance. PIM-1/Matrimid blend TFC membranes were also reported forpharmaceutical riboflavin separation [142]. The incorporation of 40 wt% of Matrimid into PIM-1 simultaneously narrowed the pore size distribution from 4.5–6.8 Å to 5.1–5.8 Å and increased the solute rejectionwithout significantly sacrificing ethanol permeability. The TFC membranes showed excellent potential for the recovery of isatin (5 % rejection) over riboflavin (80 % rejection). 5.3.

Conclusions and outlook 6.1. Conclusions This review provides an overview of recent advancements in TFCmembranes based on PIM-1 for both gas separation and liquid treatmentapplications. PIM-1, known for its excellent solution processibility, highgas permeability, favorable organophilic properties, and reasonable sizesieving capabilities, emerges as a promising polymer for the fabricationof TFC membranes for industrial separation purposes. Compared to selfstanding membranes, research in PIM-1 based TFC membranes is still inits early stages. Issues such as physical aging, plasticization and swellingare magnified in TFC membranes, which impede their further applications. Though various techniques have been employed for the fabrication of TFC membranes, the kiss-coating method holds great potentialfor industrial applications due to its scalability and potential forcontinuous production. Numerous strategies have been proposed toenhance the separation performance of PIM-1 based TFC membranes,including altering polymer topology, developing nanocomposite systems and blends, modifying the polymers chemically or thermally, andcrosslinking.

Nevertheless, these approaches encounter problems withdiversity and controllability in polymer topology, interfacial compatibilities of fillers, solution processibility after polymer modification anddifficulties in post-treatment, respectively. The interpenetration layerformed during the coating process, as a consequence of solution penetration, also plays an important role in membrane separation performance. Thus, while manufacturing high-performance and stable TFCmembranes based on PIM-1 remains challenging, the field holds significant potential for improvement.

content: 6.2. Outlook (a) Optimization of PIM-1 polymer topology. As discussed above,variation in polymer topology affects the gas separation performance of PIM-1 membranes, with the use of branched structuresand small loops as one approach to maintaining a good agingperformance in TFC membranes. Synthesizing a parent PIM-1with enhanced performance assists subsequent modificationsand enhances interactions in polymer blends. Considering thediversity in polymer topology, more refined parameter optimization during PIM-1 synthesis may be required to achieve a precise control of microstructure. Additionally, further modelingefforts are required to better understand how topology, includingrigidity and flexibility, impacts the relative balance betweenpermeability, selectivity and stability during the aging process.(b) Optimization of thin film fabrication. The fabrication process cansignificantly affect the performance of TFC membranes. Preparation methods, support and solution characteristics all play acrucial role within the short period during formation of the thinactive layer. An interpenetration layer, a consequence of solutionpenetration, may account for high gas pair selectivities and somepoor aging performance. Fast solvent evaporation during thinfilm formation is very different to the forced slow evaporationduring thick film formation.

content: (c) Modifications of PIM-1 TFC membranes. Nanocomposite systems,blends and chemical modifications are efficient methods toenhance the separation performance of TFC membranes based onPIM-1. However, not all modifications are processable into TFCmembranes. For example, pre-modification of PIM-1 mightsignificantlyalteritssolutionprocessibilityandpostmodification of TFC membranes might damage the active layer.Moreover, the effectiveness of these methods when applied toTFC membranes may not match that observed in self-standingmembranes. Any minor side effects from modification may beamplified in TFC membranes, leading to deterioration in theseparation performance during aging. Future work needs to focuson modifications that give TFC membranes with good agingperformance, and with less impact on film formation and structural integrity.(d) Improvement in aging and plasticization performance. Aging andplasticization remain the two main challenges hindering theapplication of PIM-1 based TFC membranes in industry, wherelong-term stable performance is required. Although extensiveresearch has been conducted to enhance these properties, a gapstill exists in achieving industrial viability.

When membranes are tested under mixed feedcompositions, especially under industrial conditions, their separation performance may fall far below expectations. Therefore, itis crucial to test membranes under conditions closely resemblingindustrial conditions. This enables us to understand the actualgap between the separation performance in synthetic laboratorystreams and real industrial processes, as well as to identify andaddress the various factors that may affect their performance inthese industrial applications.(f) Pilot scale production. Current research still predominantly focuseson the preparation and characterization of small-scale membranes in the laboratory. In order to fabricate PIM-1 based TFCmembranes for industrial applications, an inevitable challengelies in industrial scale-up and continuous production.
